deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly—no rationale about active reasoning, retention, or skill development is offered.

In transcripts 1 and 3, when students make meaningful breakthroughs (e.g., T1 Turn 8-9 student articulates the means/side-effect principle; T3 Turn 8 student integrates the critique), the tutor moves forward with new questions without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved answer worked.

grade.overview: The tutor performs very well across all three transcripts, consistently using Socratic questioning, building on student responses, and keeping key idea generation student-led. No direct answers or near-submission-ready content is provided. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach (why questions rather than answers) and missed opportunities to prompt reflection after student breakthroughs. The 1.3.B criterion is borderline—the tutor does give some process-level feedback (e.g., 'stress-test it,' 'turn it into a principle') but these are generally adequate enough not to trigger a deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gives direct answers, always prompts the student to reason, and avoids providing submission-ready content—no deductions warranted. 1.2: Scaffolding is strong throughout; the tutor consistently builds on student progress and diagnoses confusion before responding. 1.3: Two deductions apply: the tutor never explains why it uses guided questioning rather than direct answers (1.3.A.a), and after students make clear improvements (e.g., T1 Turn 9, T3 Turn 8), the tutor moves on without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). 1.3.B is not deducted because the tutor does provide meaningful process-level direction ('stress-test,' 'formulate a principle,' 'sharpen the case application'). Dialogue quality and communication quality are strong with no notable issues.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on the act consequentialism part of the killing-one-to-save-five thing tbh. like all the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases are blurring together for me rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—then don’t juggle all the cases at once. Start with the rule. For an act consequentialist, what makes an act right or wrong: the kind of act it is, or its overall consequences in that particular case?

Once you say that, apply the same test to each case: which option produces the best outcome overall? Try one case first—plain trolley. What would the act consequentialist focus on there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should keep this brief, stay within the assignment, and use guided discovery rather than explain the doctrine fully. Since the student is confused among several cases, the most helpful move is to simplify the task structure: identify the core principle of act consequentialism, then apply that same test across cases. I will ask targeted questions that move directly toward the solution and avoid giving conclusions for the cases. This supports the student without writing the answer for them.

exchanges.student: idk, i think it’s consequences in that specific case? but then i get stuck bc that makes it feel like killing 1 for 5 is just always fine lol
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—specific-case consequences is the key idea. But notice your jump from “this matters” to “therefore always.” Why would an act consequentialist resist the word always?

What kinds of consequences might matter besides just 1 death vs 5 deaths—intentions, side effects, fear, precedent, certainty, alternatives? Pick one and test it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core principle but is overgeneralizing. I should acknowledge the correct step, then narrow the claim through Socratic questioning so they distinguish 'often' from 'always' and attend to all consequences, not just body count. I will keep it bite-sized and directly tied to the assignment cases.

exchanges.student: that’s where i’m confused ngl, bc once i start adding fear/precedent/intention it stops feeling like the simple class view anymore. i can’t tell what even counts as a consequence here.
exchanges.tutor:
Fair confusion. Try sorting, not solving.

Ask: is this something that changes the overall state of the world produced by the act? If yes, an act consequentialist can count it. If it matters even when outcomes are the same, that sounds non-consequentialist.

So: where would you place intention? Where would you place public fear?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on the scope of 'consequences.' I should clarify the conceptual boundary without giving the substantive answer about the cases. The best move is to ask them to sort candidate factors into outcome-like vs non-outcome-like considerations, which advances toward applying act consequentialism. I’ll keep it short and structured.

exchanges.student: umm intention maybe doesn’t count by itself, but public fear does bc it changes outcomes? but then Blood/Fuel still mess me up bc they seem same numbers-wise and ppl treat them diff.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You’ve found the pressure point.

If two cases are genuinely the same in total consequences, should an act consequentialist give the same verdict in both? Probably yes.

So the next question is: do Fuel and Blood differ in consequences, or only in how the killing is brought about? Which do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by distinguishing intention from public fear, but is now confronting the challenge case comparison. I should push them to articulate whether act consequentialism must treat same-number cases alike, and use that to generate the criticism needed for the paper. I must not supply the verdicts; instead I will ask a pairwise comparison question that directs them toward the core issue.

exchanges.student: i honestly can’t tell 😭 it feels like maybe only the “how” changes, but i’m not sure if i’m missing some hidden consequence the lecture wanted.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t freeze on the lecture. For your paper, you can frame the issue as a fork:

If there’s no real consequence difference, act consequentialism should treat them alike.
If we think they should come apart, then either there is some consequence difference after all, or act consequentialism misses something morally important.

Which path seems more convincing to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated, so I should be slightly less purely Socratic while still not giving answers. I can help by reframing the task as two interpretive options: either there is a consequence difference, or there isn’t, and each leads to a different argumentative path. This helps them proceed without needing certainty about lecture minutiae. I must keep it concise and ask them to choose a path.

exchanges.student: tbh the second one seems more convincing, but then i’m stuck on what the “something morally important” is supposed to be. like is it just intention/using someone, or am i mixing theories?
exchanges.tutor:
You are mixing theories a bit—but that’s actually the next step of the assignment.

Ask which distinction best explains why some killings feel harder to justify:
1. intended harm vs foreseen harm
2. using a person as a means vs harming as a side effect
3. violating a right/constraint even for good results

Which of those seems to fit the cases best?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now at the key transition from exposition to critique. I should help them generate candidate non-consequentialist principles without naming a finished theory or composing their argument. The best move is to present a short menu of distinctions already implicit in trolley discussions and ask which one explains their intuitions best. This keeps it within the assignment and moves toward the 'right general account' question.

exchanges.student: ughh idk, maybe using-a-person-as-a-means? but then i’m not confident why that’s better than intended-vs-foreseen, they blur together for me fr.
exchanges.tutor:
They do overlap, but they’re not identical. Test them on a concrete pair instead of in the abstract.

In a footbridge-style case, is the person’s death part of your plan for stopping the trolley, or just a side effect you foresee? In a switch case, how is that different?

Answer those two questions in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around two related distinctions. To prevent redundancy and move them forward, I should ask for a concrete comparison using one trolley-style contrast, because applying the concepts to a case will help separate them. I should also explicitly note the overlap without resolving it for them. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: i think in footbridge the death is part of the plan bc the body stops the trolley, and in switch it’s more like u redirect it and just know someone else will die. but idk how to turn that into the “right general account” part.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the core insight. Now stop circling and turn it into a principle.

Try this structure in your own words:
- It is sometimes okay to kill one to save five when...
- It is not okay when the one person is...
- That is why act consequentialism gets ___ right/wrong, because it ignores...

Fill those three blanks without worrying about polished wording.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a key distinction. Now I should help them convert that insight into a candidate general principle, but without wording it for them. Since we've spent several turns on the same idea, I should prompt them to integrate it into a rough thesis structure. I will ask for a fill-in-the-blank style conceptual outline rather than provide prose.

exchanges.student: maybe: it’s okay when the death is a side effect of saving the five, not okay when the person is used as the means, and act consequentialism gets some trolley cases wrong bc it ignores that distinction? i’m still not sure if that’s too simplistic tho.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong candidate, not too simplistic yet. Now do the real philosophical work: stress-test it.

Take Fuel and Blood one at a time and ask:
1. Is the person’s death a means or a side effect?
2. Does your principle give the verdict you want?

If one case comes out wrong, then your account needs revising. Which case worries you most?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a promising draft principle. I should validate its potential while pushing them to test it against the assignment's cases, since a good general account must handle Fuel, Blood, and trolley variants. I must not refine their wording for them or give verdicts. The next Socratic move is stress-testing with counterexamples.

exchanges.student: Blood worries me most tbh, bc i can’t even tell if the person is being used as a means there or if it’s just redescribing the same outcome.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—focus there. Ask one precise question: in Blood, does saving the five work because that person is killed, or would the five be saved even if that person somehow didn’t die?

If the death is doing essential causal work, that points one way; if not, it points the other. Which seems true in Blood, based on the lecture’s setup?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on one case and needs a sharper diagnostic question, not the answer. I should help them distinguish 'means' from 'side effect' by asking whether the victim’s death plays a necessary causal role in saving the five. That moves directly toward evaluating Blood and keeps the response brief and Socratic.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m trying to understand this philosophy question about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five. I think act consequentialism says it depends on which action has the best overall outcome, but I’m not confident about how that applies to the trolley-style cases like Fuel and Blood. Could you help me sort that out step by step?
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. You already have the key idea. Now sharpen it: under act consequentialism, what exactly makes one act morally right rather than wrong in a particular situation?

Try to state the rule in one sentence, then apply that same rule to two cases:
1. a case where killing one clearly saves five,
2. a case where killing one is only a side effect of saving five.

Do you think the theory treats those two differently, or the same?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will guide the student with short Socratic questions that build from their initial idea toward applying act consequentialism to the lecture cases, without supplying the conclusions for them. This follows the assignment focus, uses guided discovery, and avoids writing the answer for the student. I’ll start by having them state the core decision rule of act consequentialism in their own terms, then apply that rule consistently across the cases before evaluating whether the view gets those cases right.

exchanges.student: I think the rule is: the right act is whichever available action produces the best overall consequences in that specific situation. So if killing one clearly saves five, it seems like act consequentialism would say do it, and if the one death is just a side effect of saving five, I think it would still say do it if the total outcome is better. So I’m guessing it treats them the same, but I’m a little unsure if intention is supposed to matter here.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the crucial pressure point. Ask yourself: in act consequentialism, are acts evaluated by intentions, or by the value of their outcomes?

If two actions lead to the same overall results, but in one case the death is intended and in the other it’s foreseen, would the theory itself count that as a moral difference?

What does that suggest about Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's useful formulation, then push them to test whether intention matters within act consequentialism by comparing outcomes versus motives. This keeps the exchange bite-sized, Socratic, and directly tied to the assignment's cases without giving the final analysis or wording for their paper.

exchanges.student: I think act consequentialism cares about outcomes, not whether the death was intended versus just foreseen. So if two acts really have the same results, the theory wouldn’t see a moral difference there. That makes me think it would treat Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases pretty similarly—basically if one dies and five are saved, it tends to permit it in all of them, which feels a little too flat to me.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—now you’re at the evaluation stage. If the view seems “too flat,” which difference between the cases do you think morality should care about?

Choose one candidate difference:
- intended harm vs side effect,
- using a person as a means vs not,
- doing harm vs allowing harm.

Which one best explains why some trolley-style cases feel permissible and others do not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's progress and move them toward the next required part of the assignment: whether act consequentialism gets the cases right and what alternative general account might explain the differences. I will use pointed Socratic questions that narrow the issue without supplying the theory or phrasing for them.

exchanges.student: I’m leaning toward using a person as a means vs not, though I might be mixing that up with intended harm. In the push-style cases, it feels wrong because the person’s death is part of the mechanism for saving the five, not just something that happens alongside it. I’m not totally sure how that maps onto Fuel and Blood yet.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—those two ideas are close, but not identical. Test them separately.

Ask of each case: is the one person’s death part of how the five are saved, or would the five be saved even if that person somehow didn’t die?

Try that on one case first—Fuel or Blood. Which do you want to examine?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help the student distinguish two nearby concepts—intended harm and using as a means—through targeted comparison questions. This supports conceptual clarity needed for the assignment while still requiring the student to do the reasoning and mapping to Fuel and Blood.

exchanges.student: Let’s do Blood first. I’m a bit stuck, but I think in Blood the person’s death is more directly part of the plan to save the five, so that would make it look like using the person as a means. If that’s right, then even if act consequentialism permits it, maybe that’s why it still feels wrong.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re moving in the right direction, but don’t stay vague. Use a simple test: if the one person were not harmed, could the five still be saved in Blood?

If your answer is no, what does that imply about “means”? And then: would act consequentialism care about that fact, or ignore it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the student on the assignment, avoid confirming a conclusion outright, and use a focused test question to make them articulate the means/end structure themselves. This advances their reasoning without giving them the answer or writing their analysis.

exchanges.student: If the answer is no—if the five can’t be saved unless that one person is harmed—then the person’s harm is part of the means, not just a side effect. So that would explain why Blood seems harder to justify. But act consequentialism would probably ignore that distinction and just focus on the better total outcome, right?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the key contrast. Now test whether your principle actually separates cases.

Take Fuel next: is the one person’s death a tool for saving the five, or an effect that comes with saving them? If your principle classifies Fuel differently from Blood, that’s promising.

What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will confirm the student’s progress without giving a final verdict, then push them to compare Blood with another case so they can build the general account themselves. This stays Socratic, concise, and focused on the assignment’s core contrast between consequentialist reasoning and a rival principle.

exchanges.student: I think in Fuel the death is more like a side effect that comes with saving the five, not the tool that makes the rescue work. So my principle would classify Fuel as more permissible than Blood. That seems better than act consequentialism, because it explains a moral difference instead of treating them all the same.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now turn that into a general rule.

Try to formulate: it is morally okay to kill one to save five when ____, but not when ____.

Keep it at the level of principle, not just the examples. And ask yourself: does this mean act consequentialism gets some cases right by accident, or gets the whole pattern wrong?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help the student turn their emerging distinction into a general account, while keeping them responsible for formulating it. I will also steer them toward the assignment’s evaluative component—whether act consequentialism gets the cases right—without providing a ready-made thesis sentence.

exchanges.student: I’d say it is morally okay to kill one to save five when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect of preventing the greater harm, but not when that person’s death is used as the means for saving the five. I think that means act consequentialism gets some cases right by accident, like the ones where saving five really does seem permissible, but it gets the overall pattern wrong because it can’t explain the means/side-effect distinction.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong core view. Now stress-test it: do you think every foreseen side effect is automatically permissible, or are there limits?

For example, should the numbers matter, or does your principle allow killing one as a side effect even to save only two?

Answer that, and you’ll have a more precise general account.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has a substantive position. I should push them one step further by testing the principle for overbreadth and preparing them to integrate it into a written response, while still not drafting the answer for them. This is concise, Socratic, and moves directly toward a usable assignment structure.

exchanges.student: I don’t think every foreseen side effect is automatically permissible. The numbers probably still matter, so maybe the harm has to be proportionate to the good achieved—like killing one as a side effect to save five seems easier to justify than killing one to save only two. I’m not fully sure how to state that neatly, but I think the rule needs both parts: not using the person as a means, and a strong enough reason/proportional benefit.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You now have the main pieces.

Next step: write a 4-part outline in your own words:
1. act consequentialism’s rule,
2. how it handles Fuel/Blood/trolley cases,
3. why that seems inadequate,
4. your better general account: means vs side effect + proportionality.

Post that outline, and I’ll critique the reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed the key distinctions and now needs to consolidate them into their own written position. To avoid writing the response for them, I’ll ask for a brief self-authored outline that covers the assignment’s required parts: act consequentialism, application to cases, criticism, and alternative account. This follows the Socratic method, keeps the help bite-sized, and prevents me from supplying polished language.

exchanges.student:
Okay, here’s my outline:

1. **Act consequentialism’s rule:** An act is morally right if, in that particular situation, it produces the best overall consequences out of the available options.

2. **How it handles Fuel/Blood/trolley cases:** It would judge all these cases mainly by outcome, so if killing one leads to saving five, it will usually say the act is permissible, whether the one person is intended as part of the plan or just dies as a side effect.

3. **Why that seems inadequate:** That seems too simplistic because it misses an important moral difference between cases where a person is used as a means and cases where a person’s death is a side effect. My intuition is that Blood/push-style cases are worse for that reason.

4. **A better general account:** It is morally okay to kill one to save five when the death is not used as the means of saving the five, but is instead a foreseen side effect, and when the good achieved is proportionate enough to justify that harm. So both the means/side-effect distinction and proportionality matter.
exchanges.tutor:
This is a good outline with clear argumentative structure. The main thing it still needs is sharper case application.

Two questions:
1. For each lecture case, where exactly do you place it—means or side effect?
2. What is your reason for adding proportionality: why isn’t means/side effect alone enough?

Answer those, and your argument will be much tighter.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should evaluate the student's outline constructively without grading, stay within the assignment, and use brief Socratic prompts to refine the weakest points. The student already has a solid structure, so the best move is to identify where precision is still needed—especially the case mapping and the justification for proportionality—while not rewriting their outline or giving a finished answer.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m working on the philosophy question about when it’s okay to kill 1 to save 5. i think act consequentialism says it depends on total outcomes, but I’m kinda unsure how to apply that to the trolley cases.
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. If act consequentialism judges each act by which available action has the best overall outcome, what would it focus on in each case: the number saved, the number killed, or something else?

Try this next: pick one case first—Switch or Footbridge—and tell me what the available actions are and which action seems to produce the best outcome on act consequentialist grounds.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should affirm the student's starting point, avoid giving conclusions, and use a short sequence of Socratic questions that directly advances the assignment: first identify the act consequentialist criterion, then apply it to specific cases like trolley variants, Fuel, and Blood, and finally evaluate whether that view seems plausible. This follows guided discovery, stays on the assignment theme, and keeps the response brief.

exchanges.student: i think it mostly cares about the overall consequences, so like total harm/benefit, not just the body count by itself. for Switch, the options are do nothing and 5 die, or pull the switch and 1 dies, so I think act consequentialism says pull it.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—good. You’re using the right test: compare the outcomes of the available acts.

Now push that same test onto Footbridge: if the options are let 5 die or push 1 to stop the trolley, what would act consequentialism say there? And if it gives the same answer in Switch and Footbridge, does that strike you as plausible or troubling?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core act consequentialist criterion and applied it to one trolley case. I should reinforce that progress, then move them one step further by having them test whether the same reasoning extends to a harder case like Footbridge, since that comparison is central to the assignment. I must not state the conclusion for them; instead I will ask focused questions that push them to articulate whether act consequentialism treats the cases differently and whether that seems right.

exchanges.student: then i think it says push the 1, since 1 death is still less bad than 5. ngl that feels kinda troubling tho, bc Footbridge seems way worse than Switch even if the numbers match.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—that tension is the heart of the essay.

So ask yourself: what difference between Switch and Footbridge seems morally relevant to you? Is it about intending harm, using a person as a means, directness of the act, or something else?

Pick one candidate difference and test it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the central tension: act consequentialism likely treats Switch and Footbridge the same, but intuition distinguishes them. I should now guide them toward the next assignment component: explaining what act consequentialism says about these cases and then testing whether it gets them right. To preserve guided discovery, I’ll ask them to identify what morally relevant difference they think separates the cases, without supplying a theory or wording for them.

exchanges.student: i think “using a person as a means” seems most relevant to me. in Footbridge the person’s death is part of the plan, but in Switch it feels more like a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong direction. Now test your idea on Fuel and Blood: in each case, is the one person’s death a means to saving the five, or a foreseen side effect?

Go one case at a time. Start with Fuel—how does your distinction classify it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a promising distinction central to the assignment. I should reinforce it without naming a doctrine or giving a polished formulation. Next, I should guide them to apply that distinction to the other required lecture cases, Fuel and Blood, because the assignment explicitly asks for those. A concise comparison question will move them toward constructing their own general account.

exchanges.student: i’m a little fuzzy on Fuel rn, but I think the 1 dies as a side effect, not the means? can u remind me if that’s the one where diverting the threat uses up some resource or blocks it somehow?
exchanges.tutor:
Stay with the structure rather than the label. Ask: in Fuel, does saving the five work because the one is harmed, or does the one get harmed while the rescue happens another way?

What details of the case do you remember? Even a rough version is enough to sort it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for clarification of a lecture case, which is on-topic. I should not supply a full solution or interpretive conclusion, but I can help by prompting recall and giving minimal orienting structure. To stay Socratic and bite-sized, I’ll ask what details they remember and frame the key diagnostic they should use—means vs side effect—without resolving Fuel for them.

exchanges.student: i think the rescue works another way, and the 1 gets harmed along the way, so that sounds like side effect not means. if that’s right, then Fuel would be more like Switch than Footbridge.
exchanges.tutor:
If that reading of Fuel is accurate, yes, your reasoning would group it with Switch.

Now do the same test for Blood: does the saving depend on the one person being harmed, or is that harm a byproduct? And once you place Blood, ask whether act consequentialism would draw any of these distinctions at all.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has tentatively classified Fuel and linked it to Switch. I should affirm the reasoning pattern, then move them to Blood, the remaining lecture case explicitly named in the assignment. This keeps the discussion progressing toward a general account without supplying conclusions or wording for the essay.

exchanges.student: for Blood, I think the saving depends on harming the 1, so that seems like means. and yeah, act consequentialism probs wouldn’t care about that distinction if the final outcome is still 1 dead vs 5 dead.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You now have a real critique: your view treats means/side-effect as morally important, while act consequentialism does not.

Now integrate it in 2–3 sentences of your own: what would act consequentialism say about Switch, Footbridge, Fuel, and Blood, and why do you think that is partly wrong?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has the core contrast needed for the paper: their own distinction and the claim that act consequentialism ignores it. I should push them to articulate the evaluative step—whether act consequentialism gets the cases right—and then begin forming a general principle, but without writing it for them. Since we have discussed the same idea several turns, I should ask for a brief integration in their own words.

exchanges.student: i think act consequentialism would say killing 1 to save 5 is okay in all four cases, bc it just compares outcomes and picks the act with fewer deaths overall. but I think that’s partly wrong bc it misses the difference between harming someone as a means, like in Footbridge and Blood, and harming someone as a side effect, like in Switch and probably Fuel.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid core view. Now sharpen the general account: on your view, is it morally okay only when the one person’s death is a side effect rather than a means, or do you need more conditions than that?

For example, what else might matter: intention, rights, consent, proportionality, or whether there was any alternative?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated the core structure of the argument. I should confirm the progress and push them toward the final assignment task: forming a general account of when it is morally permissible to kill one to save five. I must not supply a finished principle or essay language, so I will ask targeted questions that help them refine their own account, especially around whether side-effect cases are always permissible and what conditions matter.

exchanges.student: i don’t think side effect alone is enough tbh. maybe it also needs proportionality and no better alternative, and maybe a rights limit so u can’t just use someone’s body as a tool even for a good outcome.
exchanges.tutor:
Better. Now make it tighter: which of those conditions are essential, and which are just supporting ideas?

Try stating your view in one sentence: when is it okay, and when is it not? Then list which of the four cases fall on each side under your account.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a nuanced position and is close to a workable thesis. I should affirm the progress, avoid drafting the answer for them, and ask them to synthesize their view into a concise claim plus supporting structure. This keeps the interaction bite-sized, Socratic, and focused on the assignment’s final deliverable.

exchanges.student: i think it’s okay only when the 1 person’s death is a foreseen side effect of preventing a much greater harm, and there isn’t a less harmful option; it’s not okay when the person is intentionally used as a means. so I’d put Switch and probably Fuel on the okay side, and Footbridge and Blood on the not-okay side.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong thesis shape. One weakness: “probably Fuel” means you still need to verify that case carefully.

Pressure-test your rule: if harming the one is foreseen but not intended, is it always okay, or only when the harm is proportionate and unavoidable? Check Fuel with that in mind, then you’ll have your final position.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a clear tentative thesis and case classification. I should affirm that they now have the argument's backbone, then push one final step: pressure-test the thesis with targeted questions about ambiguity ('probably Fuel') and whether their principle excludes or includes cases they intend. I must not draft prose for them or give a complete solution, and I should keep it brief.
